text: Hello everyone. This is a video presentation of local excision for rectal cancer performed using DaVinci X robotic platform.

This is a seventy nine year old female who was referred with change in bowel habits. Her endoscopy revealed a three centimeter lesion with central depression, which was about seven centimeters from the anal margin. The endorectal ultrasound scan revealed this to be a T one and not lesion.

The patient had a BMI of thirty and her comorbidities included hypertension and hiatus hernia. The MRI revealed a posterior lesion. In the sagittal view, you can see it's almost next to the sphincter complex.

I'm going to briefly talk about our theatre setup as this is a crucial step for the robotic platform. The patient's legs are placed in the stirrups. They are extended and abducted so as to avoid the clash with robotic arms. The patient's side guard comes from the patient's left shoulder. We use a gel point port. The three ports are used by the robotic arms and the fourth port is an air seal port, which is used by the assistant to control the suction during the surgery. This picture shows our setup of robotic platform. The camera is at the top with robotic instruments on either side. An eight millimeter air seal port is used for interpolation and as an assistant port.
The surgeon begins the procedure by marking the margins circumferentially around the rectal lesion, leaving approximately one centimeter of healthy tissue from the lesion.

The surgeon performs a full thickness excision up to the level of the mesorectum, revealing the yellow mesorectal fat in the background. The dissection continues circumferentially around the lesion, using scissors and fenestrated bipolar forceps to carefully handle the tissue.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lesion, washes the surgical area, and checks for hemostasis. The defect is then closed using a V-lock suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.

The specimen is fixed on the polystyrene board before being sent for pathology. So far, we've done a total of five cases, all of which have been for cancer. Length of stay has been around two days and operative time similar to our TEMPS platform approach being around eighty-five minutes. There was only one patient with R one resection due to high-grade dysplasia at the margin. We've had two readmissions with rectal bleed of which one was due to diverticulosis. Flexible sigmoidoscopy in that patient did not reveal any bleeding at the site of excision. There were two complications, one patient requiring blood transfusion and another one having perianal necrosis, which we believe was from short course radiotherapy that the patient had received prior to local excision. Overall, we believe local excision can be performed safely with Da Vinci platform. It offers the advantage of precise movements of instruments, endoress technology, and three-D stereoscopic vision.
